text: Source: FAO – Wheat Water Management https://www.fao.org/land-water/databases-and-software/crop-information/wheat/en/ [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p1] Seasonal water requirement (ETm) for wheat ranges from 450 to 650 mm depending on climate and length of growing period. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p2] Crop coefficient (Kc) values for wheat are: - Initial stage (15–20 days): 0.3–0.4 - Development stage (25–30 days): 0.7–0.8 - Mid-season stage (50–65 days): 1.05–1.2 - Late-season stage (30–40 days): 0.65–0.7 - At harvest: 0.2–0.25 [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p3] Under non-stress conditions, wheat can use 50–60% of available soil water before irrigation is required. Moderate stress occurs at 70–80% depletion and severe stress above 80% depletion. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p4] For optimal root development, the entire root zone should be filled to field capacity prior to or soon after sowing. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p5] Critical periods for water deficit in wheat are: 1) At completion of tillering and beginning of stem elongation. 2) During heading and flowering. 3) During early yield formation (grain filling). [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p6] Water deficit during flowering reduces seed number per head and yield. Yield losses during flowering cannot be recovered by irrigation applied later. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p7] Water deficit during early yield formation reduces grain weight and causes shriveled grains, especially under hot and dry wind conditions. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p8] Water deficit during ripening has only a slight effect on yield and irrigation is often discontinued during this stage to allow drying-off.

iveled grains, especially under hot and dry wind conditions. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p8] Water deficit during ripening has only a slight effect on yield and irrigation is often discontinued during this stage to allow drying-off. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p9] Normally 50–60% of total water uptake occurs from the first 0.3 m of soil depth. Nearly all water uptake occurs within the top 1.0–1.5 m of soil. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p10] Under maximum evapotranspiration of 5–6 mm/day, wheat water uptake is not significantly affected until soil water depletion exceeds 50% of total available water (p ≈ 0.5). [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p11] Where irrigation water is limited, irrigation should be scheduled to avoid water deficits during flowering and around 50–70 days after sowing for spring wheat. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p12] Over‑irrigation during vegetative growth may cause excessive vegetative growth and lodging. Heavy irrigation during late yield formation may also increase lodging risk. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p13] If sufficient soil moisture is stored in the root zone before ripening, additional irrigation during late yield formation has little effect on yield. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p14] Israel (Winter Wheat) recommendation: - Irrigate when soil water depletion in the upper 1 m reaches 100–120 mm. - If at heading depletion is less than 30 mm, irrigation can be delayed until early yield formation. - If at heading depletion exceeds 50 mm, irrigation should be applied immediately. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p15] USSR (Winter Wheat) recommendation: Maintain soil water depletion in the first 1 m soil depth below 70% of total available water.

50 mm, irrigation should be applied immediately. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p15] USSR (Winter Wheat) recommendation: Maintain soil water depletion in the first 1 m soil depth below 70% of total available water. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p16] Northern India (Spring Wheat) recommendation: Irrigation options include: - One irrigation during early vegetative stage (1a). - Two irrigations: early vegetative (1a) and flowering (2). - Three irrigations: early vegetative (1a), flowering (2), and early yield formation (early 3). - Four irrigations: early vegetative (1a), late vegetative (1c), flowering (2), and early yield formation (early 3). [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p17] Heavy autumn irrigation to fill the full root zone before winter improves winter wheat establishment in regions with limited water supply. [irrigation_wheat_fao.txt::p18] Yield response factor (Ky) indicates wheat is highly sensitive to water deficit during mid-season (flowering and grain formation).
